baseline.note: Baseline is post-SC-002 estimation. See SC_002/scope-changes.json for previous changes.

changes.batch: Client Q&A — Answers A17–A18 (critical questions Q28 and Q14)

items.trigger: A17 — Inventory in Multiple Active Offers Simultaneously (Q28)
items.clientAnswer: Yes — the same inventory line can appear in multiple active offers at the same time. Bid validation still enforces qty <= current available at time of entry. Inventory is only committed at allocation approval, not at bid entry. The system should not reserve inventory at the bid stage. At allocation approval: system must display current available quantity; if allocations exceed available, system warns Admin; Admin adjusts and makes final decision.
items.description: This confirms the system's existing design — there was never a one-offer-per-inventory-line lock. The estimation already models: (1) strict bid validation at entry checking real-time available qty (A12/SC-002, covered in #19), (2) inventory commitment only at approval (#28, 18 hrs), (3) approval workflow where Admin reviews and decides (#26, 48 hrs), (4) Admin override capability (#25, 18 hrs). The 'warn if exceeds available' behavior is standard approval validation — you cannot commit inventory without checking it exists. No new scope required.
estimationItems: #19 MudBlazor DataGrid — in-cell editing (56 hrs) — bid validation at entry already included
#25 Admin override of winning customer (18 hrs) — Admin decision authority already included
#26 Approval workflow (48 hrs) — availability check at approval is inherent to committing inventory
#28 Automatic inventory commitment on approval (18 hrs) — commit logic must verify available qty
architectureNotes: No inventory-to-offer reservation or locking mechanism needed — simplifies architecture
Bid validation checks real-time available qty (not a snapshot) — already confirmed in A12/SC-002
Allocation approval is the single control point for conflict resolution — Admin sees current state and decides
Cross-offer conflicts resolve naturally: if Offer A allocates first and commits qty, Offer B's approval step will show reduced available qty
Concurrency at approval (two Admins approving simultaneously for same inventory) is a general infrastructure concern already captured in Risk Assessment, not specific to this answer
items.riskReduction: Resolves Q28 (Phase 1 CRITICAL). Unblocks allocation workflow items #25, #26, #28 (combined 84 hrs). Simplest possible design — no reservation layer, no per-offer qty tracking.

items.trigger: A18 — Unallocated Quantity After Partial Allocation: Auto-Release (Q14)
items.clientAnswer: Option A — Auto-release. After partial allocation: allocated quantity moves to Committed, remaining quantity returns to Available inventory. Original bid marked 'Partially Allocated.' Remaining quantity is not reserved — can be bid on again, allocated to other customers, included in future offers. No special 'pending hold' logic in Phase 1.
items.description: Client chose the simplest of the three options we proposed (auto-release, hold pending, admin decides). This is the natural behavior of the system as already designed: when you commit 60 of 100, the 40 were never committed — they remain Available by definition. The 'Partially Allocated' bid status is a derived state within the existing split allocation scope (#34, 48 hrs) — if allocated_qty < bid_qty, the bid is partially allocated. No new entities, states, or workflows required.
estimationItems: #34 Partial/split allocations across customers (48 hrs) — partial allocation status is a derived state within this item
#16 State transitions — automatic on approval + manual override (24 hrs) — Available/Committed transitions already modeled
#28 Automatic inventory commitment on approval (18 hrs) — commits only allocated qty, remainder stays Available
architectureNotes: Auto-release is the default behavior — uncommitted qty was never moved out of Available
'Partially Allocated' is a computed status: bid.allocatedQty > 0 AND bid.allocatedQty < bid.requestedQty
No hold/reserve/pending pool state needed in Phase 1 — reduces state machine complexity
Remaining qty is immediately available for new bids and allocations across any offer
items.riskReduction: Resolves Q14 (Phase 1 CRITICAL). Unblocks state machine design for items #16, #22, #26, #34 (combined 138 hrs of work that depended on this answer). Risk Assessment item 'Partial/split allocation business rules unclear' is now significantly de-risked.

summary.bugBufferNote: No hours added or removed. Bug buffer unchanged at 90 hrs.
summary.note: Both critical open questions (Q14 and Q28) resolved with 0 hours impact. Client chose the simplest design options in both cases — no reservation at bid stage, auto-release after partial allocation, Admin as single decision point at approval. These answers unblock ~222 hrs of combined estimation work (items #16, #22, #25, #26, #28, #34) and significantly reduce the 'Partial/split allocation business rules unclear' risk. Only Q29 (total user count / external access) remains as a critical open question. Timeline unchanged: MVP ~3.3 months (2 devs) or ~2.2 months (3 devs). Full scope ~3.7 months (2 devs) or ~2.4 months (3 devs).
criticalQuestionsResolved: Q14 — Unallocated qty after partial allocation → Auto-release to Available (simplest option)
Q28 — Same inventory in multiple active offers → Yes, no reservation, conflicts resolved at approval
remainingCriticalQuestions: Q29 — Total user count / external customer access — infrastructure sizing depends on this
